student_name: João Guilherme da Costa e Silva
hub_subtitle: Business English técnico para reuniões com fornecedores internacionais, normas e entrevistas

lesson.menu_title: Never Back Down -- The Quarterly Project Review
lesson.menu_desc: Seis semanas de atraso e um fornecedor que lembra a promessa de outro jeito: discurso indireto, verbos de relato e a reunião de variacão de prazo -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 7 -- Never Back Down
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- João Guilherme | Aula 7 -- Never Back Down

listenings.text: João, it is Elena. I know we speak tomorrow at ten, and that is exactly why I am calling you tonight. I would rather you heard this from me than in front of my director. The reason the installation is six weeks late is not the drawings. A subcontractor lost a cable batch in April, and I was told it had been dispatched. It had not. I found out three weeks later, and I did not escalate it, because I believed we could recover it quietly. We could not. So tomorrow, when you read out the minutes, you will be right. On the third of June I did tell you the relay would ship on the fifteenth, and I meant it when I said it. The recovery schedule was never sent, and I should have called you. What I do not accept, and I will say this tomorrow as well, is that the whole variance is ours. Your interface drawings were eight days late, and my team could not start without them. Eight days of that thirty are yours. Now, what I want to ask you, and you are completely free to say no. Please do not take this to the steering committee in April. If it goes there, my company will send a lawyer and not an engineer, and you and I will stop solving this. Give me until Friday. You will have a recovery schedule, a second crew at our cost, and a call every Monday morning for as long as you want one. And João, one more thing. You have every right to be angry tomorrow. I would be.

Before you chair that review, three rules, because engineers chair these meetings badly and it costs them years. First. A review meeting does not exist to establish who is guilty. It exists to establish what is true, what has changed, and who does what by when. Blame is a by-product; the date is the product. If you walk out of that room having won the argument and having agreed nothing, you have lost, and you will be back in the same room in six weeks. Second, and this is the expensive one. Never agree a new date before you know the cause. A supplier who is late will always offer you a new date, immediately, because the new date ends the uncomfortable conversation. But a date agreed without a cause is a guess dressed up as a commitment, and it will slip a second time, on your schedule and at your cost. Ask what happened. Ask for the plan that fixes what happened. Only then talk about dates. Third. The float belongs to the project, not to the supplier. They will ask you for it. They will ask nicely, and they will call it flexibility. But the moment you hand over two weeks of float so that somebody can cover their own delay, you have paid for their mistake with time that you will need, later, when something goes wrong that nobody planned for. And something always does. And one last thing, about how you sound. You do not need to be aggressive in that meeting. You need to be precise. Read the date, name the commitment, use the word they used, and then be quiet. Silence, after a fact, does more work than anger ever will.

patterns.title: The verb you choose decides how much it binds them
cards: Neutral. It happened, that is all
You are recording, not pressing. Nobody is bound by it. "They said that the cable was on its way."
Neutral, but it names the audience
TELL always takes the person: told US, told ME. It puts the room in the sentence, so it cannot be denied privately. "Your team told us that the installation was on track."
This is the one you use when you intend to hold them to it. To assure is to remove somebody's doubt on purpose. "On 3 June you assured us that the relay would ship on the fifteenth."
The strongest of the four, and the only one with a to-infinitive. It is contract language: they took the duty on. "Your company undertook to submit a recovery schedule within ten days."

patterns.text: Three more you will need in the minutes: they CLAIMED it (you are signalling that you doubt it), they ACKNOWLEDGED or CONCEDED it (they admitted something against their own interest -- write this one down, it is gold), they DENIED it (they refused it outright). Choose the verb before you choose the tone.

items.situation: "So what is the actual problem here?" Open the review with the number. No names, no adjectives.
tips: The schedule variance on the installation is minus thirty working days.
The activity is on the critical path, so the float is zero and nothing absorbs it.

items.situation: "I never promised you the fifteenth. I said we would do our best." Answer from the record.
tips: On 3 June you assured us that the relay would ship on the fifteenth, and the minutes were circulated the same afternoon.
That is why I am reading from the minutes, and not from memory.

items.situation: "The drawings arrived late from your side." She is partly right. Concede, then hold.
tips: To some extent, both parties share responsibility: the drawings were issued eight days late.
The slippage we are discussing, however, is thirty days.

items.situation: "Give us two weeks of float from the testing activity and we recover everything." Refuse it.
tips: I am afraid the float belongs to the project, not to the package that is late.
If I hand it over now, I have nothing left when something unplanned happens later.

items.situation: "What do you need from us today?" Three things, each with a date.
tips: A recovery schedule by Friday, showing how the thirty days are won back.
A contingency plan if the retest fails, and a weekly call so the next slip reaches me in three days.

items.situation: "Can we keep this between us, and not escalate?" Say yes on your terms, or no without closing the door.
tips: If we can reach a consensus today, this does not have to go to the steering committee in April.
Without a recovery schedule, I have no position to defend, and the decision stops being mine.

bank.label: Opening with the number
items: The schedule variance on the installation is minus thirty working days.
The activity is on the critical path, so there is no float to absorb the delay.
Let me start with the number, and then I would like to hear you.

bank.label: Putting it on the record
items: On 3 June you assured us that the relay would ship on the fifteenth.
In the last review your team told us that the installation was on track.
Your company undertook to submit a recovery schedule within ten days.

bank.label: Conceding without giving way
items: To some extent, both parties share responsibility for the interface drawings.
One could argue that the delay was partly caused by the late drawings. The slippage, however, is thirty days.
That is fair, and I will record it. It does not change what is on the critical path.

bank.label: Closing with commitments
items: A recovery schedule by Friday, and a contingency plan if the retest fails.
If we can reach a consensus today, this does not have to go to the steering committee.
Before we close, let me summarize what each side has undertaken to do.
